original_abstract: . There is large consent that successful training of deep net- works requires many thousand annotated training samples. In this pa- per, we present a network and training strategy that relies on the strong use of data augmentation to use the available annotated samples more eﬃciently. The architecture consists of a contracting path to capture context and a symmetric expanding path that enables precise localiza- tion. We show that such a network can be trained end-to-end from very few images and outperforms the prior best method (a sliding-window convolutional network) on the ISBI challenge for segmentation of neu- ronal structures in electron microscopic stacks. Using the same net- work trained on transmitted light microscopy images (phase contrast and DIC) we won the ISBI cell tracking challenge 2015 in these cate- gories by a large margin. Moreover, the network is fast. Segmentation of a 512x512 image takes less than a second on a recent GPU. The full im- plementation (based on Caﬀe) and the trained networks are available at http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net. 1
humanized_abstract: There is a wide consensus that training a deep neural network strongly needs large amounts of labeled training data, amounting to thousands of samples. Here we show that highly accurate, full-resolution, segmentation can be obtained by strongly exploiting data augmentation when only a few images are available for training. In our approach, an encoding network path is used for context learning and a symmetric decoding path for localization of the output. This network is end-to-end trainable and we demonstrate that using it we can train and apply segmentation models to train on very few samples (less than 10 images per class). On the ISBI challenge dataset for neural cell segmentation in stacks of EM images our model reaches performance figures which significantly outclass the competition winner, namely a sliding-window based CNN. Using the same base model but initially trained with samples taken from the images of the ISBI 2015 “cell tracking challenge” - which involved transmitted light microscopy images, (phase contrast and DIC) - our results of cell tracking scored first prize in the mentioned challenge in these modality categories. The network segments in about less than one second large images with modern hardware. Our full Caﬀe-based implementation including the pre-trained networks for the applied use cases are available from http://lmb.informatik.uni-freiburg.de/people/ronneber/u-net. 1
